title: Roadkill

jokes.text: David expressing a 'deep, aching sense of dread' about leaving someone to watch his store
jokes.setup: David describes an existential feeling of anxiety
jokes.punchline: The cause is something as mundane as leaving someone to watch his shop for a day — his dramatic language is wildly disproportionate
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose character comedy — his overblown emotional vocabulary applied to a trivial situation. Works as an opener because it immediately establishes his neurotic personality.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Tax seminars? You're going to tax seminars?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I have to learn about taxes, David. I can't just ignore them forever.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why not? I have been for years.

jokes.text: David: 'Why did Patrick have to book today of all days for his tax seminar? Like who voluntarily goes to a tax seminar?' Alexis: 'Maybe the kinds of people who want to keep their businesses?'
jokes.setup: David complains petulantly about Patrick attending a tax seminar as if it's an act of betrayal
jokes.punchline: Alexis delivers a perfectly logical, deadpan burn that also doubles as self-aware commentary on David's business incompetence
jokes.explanation: Tight construction. David's complaint is so illogical it sets up a perfect straight-man retort. Alexis's line does double duty: it's funny on its face and it reveals something true about David's casual relationship with running a business. Economy is nearly perfect — one line demolishes him.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A Tamagotchi? Really?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis. It's literally just a small digital pet that you have to keep alive by feeding it and taking care of it.

jokes.text: David giving Alexis an elaborate set of instructions just to receive a package, including 'unless there's an emergency. And then immediately call—' before Alexis cuts him off knowing he means Patrick
jokes.setup: David is giving Alexis instructions as if she's incapable of babysitting his store
jokes.punchline: Alexis already knows his emergency contact is Patrick, not her — undercutting his instructions and revealing the absurdity of the entire arrangement
jokes.explanation: Solid sibling dynamic joke. Alexis finishing David's thought underscores how well she knows him while also mocking the absurdity of his preparedness. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was just trying to keep them alive!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You had seventeen of them, Alexis. Seventeen. And you named them all variations of your own name.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, Alexiss with two S's, Lex, Lexi, Lexus — which, by the way, is a car — Lex-Anne with a hyphen, A-Lex, X-Lex, and then you just gave up and named one 'Alexis But Make It Fashion.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That last one was ironic.

jokes.text: The Tamagotchi argument: 'You left me with six of them, David. Taking care of that many is like a full-time job!' — 'I left you with six adult Tamagotchis in perfect health!'
jokes.setup: David is worried about leaving Alexis in charge, referencing a past failure
jokes.punchline: The failure turns out to be killing six Tamagotchis — David treats this with the gravity of a genuine tragedy, specifying they were 'adults in perfect health'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The specificity ('six adult Tamagotchis in perfect health') is what elevates this from a simple absurdist joke to a character revelation. David's moral seriousness about a digital pet murder is perfectly calibrated. 'You have to actively murder them for that to happen' treats the inanimate toys as real victims. Highly quotable and deeply specific to David's character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're actively murdering these digital pets.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not murdering them, David. They're not real.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They may not be real, but your negligence is very real.

jokes.text: Stevie explaining she's at the flea market to escape rehearsals with Moira: 'Last night she texted me at 3 A.M. and all it said was "leggings."'
jokes.setup: Stevie explains why she volunteered to help David at the flea market instead of rehearsing
jokes.punchline: A single cryptic 3 AM text — 'leggings' — communicates Moira's obsessive, unhinged director energy perfectly without further explanation
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The comedy is entirely in specificity and economy — 'leggings' as a standalone 3 AM text from Moira tells us everything about her character without explanation. It's funnier the more you know Moira. The audience fills in the absurd internal logic. One of the best jokes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I need a break from Moira. She texted me 'k' this morning.

jokes.text: Moira describing Stevie as 'quite the find' who will need 'ballet intensive, perhaps some mittens to curb the nail biting'
jokes.setup: Moira is ostensibly on a relaxing spa day with John
jokes.punchline: She's already mentally redesigning Stevie's physical habits and body — treating her like a fixer-upper project rather than a capable adult
jokes.explanation: Strong Moira character comedy. The specificity of 'mittens to curb the nail biting' is a lovely detail — she's thought about this. It simultaneously reveals her controlling nature and her genuine (if misguided) investment in Stevie's development.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not going to stop talking about Tamagotchis. I didn't murder someone.

jokes.text: Moira promises to enjoy the spa, then immediately calculates the precise timing: '60 minutes palpated, cucumber water, five minutes for you to use the facilities, back home by noon'
jokes.setup: John asks Moira to relax and take a break from cabaret
jokes.punchline: Her idea of 'relaxing' is scheduling every minute of the day with military precision, defeating the entire purpose of a spa day
jokes.explanation: Good Moira character work. The irony of promising to relax while instantly timetabling the relaxation is a recurring comedic truth about controlling personalities. 'Five minutes for you to use the facilities' is the standout specific detail — she's allotted him bathroom time.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Stevie, darling, I've been thinking about your future, and I have some thoughts. Perhaps you could be a car rental associate? You have the right amount of... availability. Or maybe a phlebotomist? You're so good with people, but not in a way that requires conversation. Oh! A parking enforcement officer. You have that natural authority that comes from deep-seated resentment.

jokes.text: Moira choosing between fabric swatches for a chaise lounge while riding in a car, describing them: 'Similar texture but falls apart in emotional complexity'
jokes.setup: John has told Moira to put her phone down and relax
jokes.punchline: Moira is evaluating fabric swatches using art-criticism language — 'falls apart in emotional complexity' — as if suede has feelings
jokes.explanation: Characteristically Moira — applying her theatrical vocabulary to home décor. 'Falls apart in emotional complexity' is a genuinely funny phrase that could only come from her. Reveals she cannot truly disengage from her projects.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A massage is a series of movements performed on the body using pressure and friction. The practitioner will apply varying degrees of force to manipulate the soft tissues, which may result in temporary discomfort, followed by what some describe as relief. It's essentially consensual assault with essential oils.

jokes.text: John trying to look at fabric swatches while driving, eventually agreeing to look before the cat impact
jokes.setup: Moira insists John look at the swatches while he's driving; he repeatedly refuses then gives in
jokes.punchline: The distraction leads directly to him hitting the cat — the bit escalates to an actual consequence
jokes.explanation: Well-structured escalation beat. The mundane domestic squabble (fabric swatches in the car) leading to genuine plot consequence (hitting the cat) is classic sitcom construction. Earns a rewatch bonus for being the inciting incident of the episode's main plot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Now, I've allocated 40 minutes for my Swedish massage, 30 minutes for the seaweed wrap, 20 minutes for the facial, and Johnny, I've blocked off an hour and a half for you in the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: An hour and a half?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, I've factored in time for you to stand there looking confused by all the products.

jokes.text: Moira after hitting the cat: 'Oh it's the circle of life, John. You've just provided a nice Passover Seder for a family of hawks.'
jokes.setup: John has just hit an animal and is distressed; Moira's reaction is needed
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes roadkill as a contribution to the food chain, complete with a specific religious holiday reference — wholly unbothered
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'Passover Seder for a family of hawks' is specific, unexpected, and perfectly Moira — she's reframing a small tragedy through grandiose theatrical language while being completely emotionally detached. The specificity of 'Passover Seder' rather than just 'meal' is what makes it exceptional. Only Moira could say this line.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This velvet has real emotional complexity. It's vulnerable, yet unapologetically sensual. This silk, on the other hand, is emotionally distant—it's beautiful, but it doesn't care if you love it.

jokes.text: Moira: 'John, don't get too close, dear. We can't afford you contracting mange.'
jokes.setup: John discovers they've hit a cat and goes to look
jokes.punchline: Moira's concern is not for the animal or for John's emotional state, but for him contracting a skin disease — her priorities are perfectly reversed
jokes.explanation: Tight, well-calibrated line. Moira's concern hierarchy (not: poor animal, not: are you okay John, but: mange) reveals her character economically. 'We can't afford you contracting mange' has a slight absurdist edge — as if mange is a financial concern.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you hit it, it's probably just returning to the circle of life. It's Passover for the animals.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Well, what kind of ailurophobe sends their kitty out to play on such a busy stretch of highway?'
jokes.setup: John reveals it's a cat with a collar — someone's pet
jokes.punchline: Moira uses the word 'ailurophobe' (cat-hater) incorrectly and shifts blame to the imaginary irresponsible owner, while also misdescribing a rural road as 'a busy stretch of highway'
jokes.explanation: Moira's deployment of obscure vocabulary is a signature character trait. 'Ailurophobe' is almost certainly the wrong word (an ailurophobe fears cats, they don't own them carelessly), which adds a layer — she's using a fancy word incorrectly to deflect blame. Perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I for one am not about to be made a fool of by some ailurophobe who thinks they can just waltz into our lives and claim ownership of our cat.

jokes.text: After approaching the farmhouse to return the cat, John and Moira's reaction beat when the farmer turns out to be intimidating/unwelcoming — implied by John's defeated 'Or not.'
jokes.setup: Moira spots a farmhouse and confidently says it 'probably belongs to them' — implying an easy resolution
jokes.punchline: John's single 'Or not.' after approaching implies the situation is far worse than expected
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection via economy. 'Or not.' does a lot of work — it signals the scene shift into cringe territory and sets up the farmhouse sequence. The comedy is in the gap between Moira's breezy confidence and reality.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm organizing these by color, size, and provenance. It's very important to have a system. Serial killers have systems, and look how organized they are.

jokes.text: David at the flea market telling items to face forward: 'Facing forward. See what I'm doing here? Okay, like a serial killer?' — then doing a double-take when he sees a familiar face
jokes.setup: David is hyper-controlling about product placement at the flea market booth
jokes.punchline: He compares his own meticulous arrangement to serial killer behavior — a moment of self-awareness that reveals how unhinged his standards are
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy establishing David's control issues. The serial killer comparison is slightly hacky but redeemed by his immediate self-awareness. Sets up the Wendy encounter well.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: David? David Rose? Oh my God, it IS you! What are you doing here?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Hello, Wendy. I'm just browsing.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Well, I am absolutely certain I saw you here. I'm as certain of that as I am of my marriages ending.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Which is...?

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Four times certain.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'If this isn't a full circle moment, then I'm not twice divorced.'
jokes.setup: Wendy runs into David at the flea market, excited about the reunion
jokes.punchline: She swears on her two divorces as if they're credentials or points of pride — treating personal disasters as identity markers
jokes.explanation: Excellent character introduction line. The structure mirrors the idiom 'I'm not [identity marker]' but subverts it by making the marker deeply self-deprecating. Wendy is immediately established as someone who wears her disasters with cheerful pride. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Love is like a bird...

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: ...you have to feed it, or it dies. But also, you can't keep it in a cage, or it will suffocate. But if you let it out, it might fly into a window and die anyway. So really, the bird is doomed no matter what you do, which is why I don't have pets.

jokes.text: Wendy assuming Stevie is David's wife. David and Stevie react with simultaneous 'God, no' / 'No'
jokes.setup: Wendy enthusiastically greets them and assumes they're a couple
jokes.punchline: The mutual, immediate, overlapping rejection — 'God, no' — is funnier than any elaborate denial
jokes.explanation: Classic ensemble reaction beat. The simultaneous rejection is a well-worn sitcom move but executed cleanly here. The 'God, no' phrasing has appropriate comic weight. The joke is more in the performance than the writing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So it's exactly like your old store, just with more desperation and a folding table.

jokes.text: This must be your wife. — No. — God, no. — No, no.
jokes.setup: Wendy sees David with Stevie and assumes they're a couple
jokes.punchline: The triple denial — 'No.' / 'God, no.' / 'No, no.' — escalates the rejection of the suggestion with increasing horror
jokes.explanation: The escalating triple denial is a classic comedic structure executed well. 'God, no.' from Stevie or David carries the most weight. The rhythm is tight and lands reliably.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, Antonio does have a certain... je ne sais quoi. Though I'm not sure if it's the same je ne sais quoi that I have. Mine is more of a refined, understated elegance, whereas his seems to be more of a... how do I put this delicately... aggressive desperation.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'You know what they say about love, it's like target practice, if you just keep shooting at it...' [trails off]
jokes.setup: Wendy announces she's engaged again (despite being twice divorced)
jokes.punchline: She produces a mangled metaphor about love as target practice that doesn't quite land even within the scene — she can't finish it
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the unfinished metaphor — Wendy starts with confidence and the ellipsis reveals she doesn't know where she's going. The 'keep shooting at it' framing works on multiple levels (persistence, violence, luck). Her inability to complete her own wisdom is characterful.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Right, Karen.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

jokes.text: David: 'I know, I am as shocked as you are.' (about owning a business with a partner)
jokes.setup: Wendy is impressed that David owns a business with a partner
jokes.punchline: David admits he finds his own success surprising — a rare moment of self-deprecating honesty that undercuts any pride
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for David — his growth is real but his self-awareness is still filtered through self-deprecation. 'I am as shocked as you are' is economical and honest. Works best in context of knowing David's arc.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She has terrible taste in men. She once told me she thought Jon Gosselin was attractive.

jokes.text: David, after meeting Antonio: 'Well, isn't that a compliment. For him.' (when Wendy says Antonio reminds her of David)
jokes.setup: Wendy says Antonio reminds her of David, clearly meaning it as a compliment
jokes.punchline: David redirects the compliment entirely to Antonio — implying the comparison flatters Antonio, not himself, with perfect dry delivery
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. The structure is perfect — David appears to accept the compliment before the second clause reveals he's done the opposite. 'For him' is three syllables of devastating self-deprecation and simultaneous self-aggrandizement. Highly quotable, great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is this a package for me? I'm not expecting anything. Did I order something and black out?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, no. I'm not here as a delivery person. I actually... I bought you something.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, you bought me a gift? Ted, that's so sweet!

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Karen's a new one.'
jokes.setup: Wendy has just called her 'Karen' and walked away
jokes.punchline: Stevie's response implies she's been misnamed before but this particular name is a new addition to the collection
jokes.explanation: The callback is immediate but the line has more going on — it implies Stevie accepts being misnamed as a regular occurrence, adding texture to her character. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're here with my package?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, I have your delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, come in, come in. I wasn't expecting you so soon.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I aim to please.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Your robe—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I'm so embarrassed. I just got out of the shower. Don't look at me!

jokes.text: David: 'She has the worst taste in men. At one point, she told me she was looking for a Jon Gosselin type.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie gossip about Wendy's fiancé after she leaves
jokes.punchline: David reveals Wendy's ideal man is Jon Gosselin — a very specific, very unfortunate celebrity reference that tells us everything about her taste
jokes.explanation: The Jon Gosselin reference is a very specific (if slightly dated) punchline. Works well because it implies a complete character portrait of Wendy through a single cultural shorthand. Funny but shelf-life may be limited.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Hello, I am here to deliver your package. I have filled out all the requisite forms in triplicate and I have my delivery confirmation device right here. Should I place it on the counter or would you prefer I leave it by the door? I should note that according to subsection 4.2 of the delivery company handbook, I am required to obtain a signature.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm being sexy. Is it not working?

jokes.text: Alexis sarcastically telling an unseen customer 'Sorry, we're closed' — the 'customer' turns out to be Alexis's boyfriend Ted
jokes.setup: Alexis is bored waiting for a delivery; she hears someone at the door
jokes.punchline: She sarcastically says 'we're closed' expecting it to be someone else; it's Ted, who responds 'Very funny' — revealing she knew who it was
jokes.explanation: Light setup joke. Works mainly as a transition to establish Ted's arrival and set up the roleplay sequence. Not particularly inventive but efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was late because my dog died that morning.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, I'm so sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you didn't seem very sorry when you were handcuffing me to the bed saying 'Daddy's here to punish the naughty delivery boy.'

jokes.text: Ted arriving as a 'delivery man' bringing Alexis gifts he bought at the flea market — beginning an elaborate delivery man roleplay
jokes.setup: Ted shows up pretending to be a delivery man with a gift
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately plays along with a barely-veiled sexual roleplay scenario while David's store is supposedly under her care
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the contrast between Alexis's solemn promise to David and her immediate abandonment of responsibility. The roleplay itself is broad but the character irony is the real joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I think we should just be honest about what happened with the accident.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Honest? Johnny, honesty is a luxury for people with good insurance and strong cheekbones. We were hit by a vehicle piloted by a woman named Brenda who was distracted by her emotional support llama.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The llama had escaped from a traveling circus, and Brenda was a former trapeze artist trying to recapture her glory days. She didn't even have a license—she was operating the vehicle illegally as part of an underground car-sharing operation run by her estranged mother.

jokes.text: Ted, in character as delivery man: 'Uh...miss, seems that the strap has fallen off your shoulder. I'd offer to put it back for you, but as a delivery guy, I don't know if it's my place.'
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are doing a delivery man roleplay
jokes.punchline: Ted's scripted concern about professional boundaries while clearly participating in a seduction scenario is absurdly earnest — he's very committed to the premise
jokes.explanation: Ted's over-earnestness is his defining trait and it plays perfectly in the roleplay context. His concern about professional propriety as a fake delivery man is a funny expression of his character — he can't help but be respectful even when the scenario doesn't require it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have been informed that my husband has engaged in sexual relations with our son's former paramour.

jokes.text: Alexis, still in character: 'Now, if only you had that much respect for my time. I've been waiting here forever.' Ted: 'Sorry that I'm late... my... grandmother passed this morning.'
jokes.setup: Alexis demands Ted explain his lateness within the roleplay
jokes.punchline: Ted, clearly improvising, kills off his grandmother — an absurdly dark and inappropriate story for a sexy roleplay
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. Ted's improvised grandmother death is perfectly wrong for the context — he's committed enough to the roleplay to make up a story, but the story kills the mood completely. Alexis's horrified 'Ew! Oh my God, Ted!' is the perfect reaction. The line 'I wasn't prepared for an improv' is a great character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I've been practicing what I call 'vehicular decorum' — it's a very delicate balance of grace and restraint while operating a motor vehicle.

jokes.text: Sorry that I'm late... my... grandmother passed this morning.
jokes.setup: Ted is in delivery-man roleplay; Alexis has asked why he's late
jokes.punchline: Ted's improvised excuse — a freshly dead grandmother — is both inappropriately dark and delivered with visible hesitation, making it funnier
jokes.explanation: The dark turn to 'grandmother passed this morning' is funny because it's wildly inappropriate for the context and Ted clearly didn't plan it. His visible discomfort at his own choice ('my... grandmother passed') makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I would love to stay and chat, but I have a very important appointment at the spa. I'm getting a seaweed wrap, followed by a hot stone massage, and then I'm having my chakras aligned by a shaman who only works on Tuesdays.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Plus, I promised myself I'd be home in time to practice my interpretive dance routine. It's very demanding work, and quite frankly, your cat situation, while tragic, is simply not as pressing as my wellness schedule.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Sorry, I wasn't prepared for an improv.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is horrified by Ted's grandmother death improvisation
jokes.punchline: Ted breaks character to explain he wasn't ready to improvise — treating the sexy roleplay like a theatrical exercise he needed to rehearse
jokes.explanation: This is a gem. 'I wasn't prepared for an improv' treats a spontaneous sexy scenario like a theatrical form requiring advance notice. It's both meta (commenting on improv as a craft) and deeply specific to Ted's earnest, over-prepared personality. The line reframes the entire preceding bit perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose we could pop in for a moment. Johnny, darling, why don't you stay here and... chat with the livestock.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to the spa. Alone. Without you.

jokes.text: Ted, when asked what else he wasn't prepared for: 'The... import taxes?'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks what else Ted wasn't prepared for in the roleplay
jokes.punchline: Ted offers 'import taxes' — a completely non-sequitur answer that makes no sense in the romantic or the delivery-man context
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of 'import taxes' as his answer is funny precisely because it's so wrong. Ted's brain goes to logistics and bureaucracy even when trying to be romantic. Good character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait. Is this... are you selling... knockoffs?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're selling counterfeit goods out of my store?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, technically it's our store now.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That is not the point! This is illegal!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Moira preparing John's cover story for the farmer: 'You and I just innocently witnessed an 18-wheeler barreling down...'
jokes.setup: John wants to be honest with the farmer about hitting the cat
jokes.punchline: Moira has already constructed an elaborate fictional backstory involving an 18-wheeler — she defaults to performance and fabrication
jokes.explanation: Good Moira character beat. Her instinct is always to construct a narrative. The 18-wheeler detail is specific enough to be funny — she's already cast them as witnesses rather than perpetrators. John's pragmatic interruption works as a straight-man beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A sunflower? Really? That's what we're going with? Sunflowers are so... cheerful. And common. They're the participation trophy of flowers.

jokes.text: Moira to the farmer: 'My husband struck down your feline with our car.'
jokes.setup: John has been trying to carefully and politely approach the subject of the dead cat
jokes.punchline: Moira blurts it out in full, direct, and slightly theatrical language — the opposite of the delicate approach John intended
jokes.explanation: Moira undermines John's careful approach by just saying it. 'Struck down your feline' is the perfect Moira phrasing — formal, slightly biblical, completely tactless. The joke is in the contrast between John's diplomatic buildup and her brutal bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Wait, which David did I talk to?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The one that's literally standing right here.

jokes.text: John: 'Well, we don't necessarily know whether the cat belongs to this, uh, gentleman.' Farmer: 'Well, what did it look like?' John: 'The name on the tags was Sergio?' Farmer: 'Well, that was him.'
jokes.setup: John hedges about whether the cat belongs to this farmer
jokes.punchline: The farmer confirms it's his cat through the name 'Sergio' — the very specific name doing comedic work, followed by John's defeat
jokes.explanation: Simple but functional. The name 'Sergio' for a farm cat is unexpectedly specific and the farmer's flat 'Well, that was him' is perfect deadpan. John's attempt to create ambiguity is immediately deflated.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These are just knockoff versions of the real thing. They're cheap imitations that don't even come close to the quality.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny - you and your products are more alike than you think.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is that supposed to mean?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you're both kind of fake too.

jokes.text: Farmer: 'Oh, these city people driving too damn fast up and down this road.' John: 'No, I-I don't think speed was the thing here.' Moira: 'No, my husband has what's called a feather foot.'
jokes.setup: The farmer accuses them of driving too fast
jokes.punchline: Moira defends John by introducing 'feather foot' as if it's a recognized medical or automotive term — implying he's so gentle with the pedal it's basically a medical condition
jokes.explanation: Moira inventing a term ('feather foot') and presenting it with institutional authority is a classic Moira move. It's simultaneously an insult to John's driving capability and a defense of him. The phrase itself is original and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm just going to leave. I'm going to get in the car and I'm going to drive away from here and never come back.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And I'm going to change my name, and I'm going to start a new life somewhere where nobody knows who I am or what happened at this flea market.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Now that's done, and we have a very pressing engagement so.' John: 'Well, my wife is so right, we're running very late right now.' Moira: 'The Hammam Spas are notoriously fickle about refunds.'
jokes.setup: They've just confessed to killing the farmer's cat
jokes.punchline: Both immediately pivot to their spa appointment as if it's an adequate reason to leave — invoking the spa's refund policy as moral justification
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe beat. John and Moira's instinct to flee via spa appointment is funny because it's true to both characters — John's social awkwardness and Moira's self-absorption. The farmer's incredulous response amplifies the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Farmer
dialogue.line: My daughter's out there somewhere, probably knee-deep in some guy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, good for her! A girl's got to get her steps in.

jokes.text: Moira after hearing they need to break bad news to the daughter: 'Hm, John, shall I pick you up on the way back? I know how desperate you were for me to have my day of detox.'
jokes.setup: The farmer says they must come inside and tell his daughter her cat is dead
jokes.punchline: Moira attempts to assign John sole responsibility for the bad news delivery while she goes to the spa — framing her abandonment as a gift to him
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira selfishness wrapped in concern for John. 'I know how desperate you were for me to have my day of detox' is a perfect inversion — she's framing her self-interest as selflessness. The absurdity of attempting this escape mid-tragedy is peak Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it was a Tuesday. Or a Wednesday. Time moves differently when you're grieving. I found Mr. Whiskers on the chaise lounge in the sunroom, perfectly posed, as if he'd orchestrated the whole thing. And perhaps he had. Perhaps he'd simply decided that this mortal coil was no longer worth the effort. It was very Schopenhauer of him, really. A philosophical statement wrapped in fur.

jokes.text: David: 'You can buy our superior foot cream at a slightly higher price.' Roland: 'Geez. You were a lot nicer when I was talking to you over there.'
jokes.setup: David tries to redirect Roland to buy from the legitimate booth
jokes.punchline: Roland prefers the knockoff David — who was apparently more pleasant — over the real David
jokes.explanation: Strong payoff. The fake David being 'nicer' than the real David is a perfect encapsulation of David's prickly personality. The joke also works as a commentary on David's relationship with customer service.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Is that... feet? Why does it smell like a foot had a foot?

jokes.text: Uh, no, you can buy our superior foot cream at a slightly higher price.
jokes.setup: Roland asks if he can have the sale on the knockoff foot cream
jokes.punchline: David's counter-offer: 'superior' product at 'slightly higher' price — his entire sales pitch is two comparative adjectives and zero enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The laconic sales pitch — 'superior... slightly higher price' — is David at his most resigned. It's excellent economy; the whole joke is in the contrast between what he SHOULD be saying and what he actually manages to produce while in crisis mode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a luxury face cream that costs $800 a jar, and the description says 'luxurious luxury for your luxurious face.'

jokes.text: Geez. You were a lot nicer when I was talking to you over there.
jokes.setup: David refuses to honor the knockoff sale and offers his own products at higher prices
jokes.punchline: Roland's complaint that the 'David' at the other table was nicer — implying Antonio's impersonation was more pleasant than the real David
jokes.explanation: Excellent punchline. The implication that David's knockoff is more pleasant than the real David lands beautifully. It's a perfect double joke — comments on David's difficult personality AND the irony of the knockoff situation. Quotable and character-specific. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These are knockoff products. This is a knockoff of my brand.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, but they're a quarter of the price!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Roland, they say 'Schitt's Creek Apothecary' on them. That's MY name.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know! Isn't it great? I'm gonna buy a whole case!

jokes.text: David and Stevie discovering that Antonio's booth is a knockoff of Rose Apothecary: David: 'What the actual fuck?!' then 'Tulips instead of roses? Woof!'
jokes.setup: Stevie tells David to look at Antonio's booth and notice similarities to their store
jokes.punchline: David's progression from outrage to aesthetic critique ('Tulips instead of roses? Woof!') — he's equally upset about the theft and the inferior design choices
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character — David's outrage at IP theft pivots immediately to design criticism. 'Woof!' as a reaction to bad aesthetic choices is very David. The moment reveals his priorities: the theft is wrong but the tulips are personally offensive.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, these products are so cheap, pharmacies are gonna start giving them out for Halloween instead of candy.

jokes.text: David to Stevie: 'How far do you think we'd get if we just started running?'
jokes.setup: David is overwhelmed by the knockoff situation and Roland's presence
jokes.punchline: David floats abandonment as a genuine strategic option — considering running away from a flea market booth as a reasonable business decision
jokes.explanation: Quick, functional joke that captures David's crisis response style (flight over fight). Works as a pressure valve moment. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That was... wow. I mean, you really knew what you were doing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, no I didn't. I was just lying there thinking about my highlights the whole time.

jokes.text: Moira asking when the daughter will appear: 'When might we expect your daughter to show up?' Farmer: 'Baby's still sleeping, but if you wanna go wake her up, by all means.' Moira: 'Where is Bebe's chamber?'
jokes.setup: Moira is eager to get the cat-death notification over with and leave
jokes.punchline: She refers to the daughter's bedroom as her 'chamber' — theatrical vocabulary applied to a farmhouse; also immediately volunteering to wake the sleeping woman to deliver terrible news
jokes.explanation: Moira's language is always slightly elevated for the situation. 'Bebe's chamber' is perfect — it's not a bedroom, it's a chamber. Also, her eagerness to wake a stranger just to tell her the cat is dead and leave is delightfully callous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, we could have been caught!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, that would have been so hot.

jokes.text: Farmer on the daughter: 'She's either up, or takin' a leak.' Moira: 'Either way, great progress for Bebe.'
jokes.setup: They hear sounds from upstairs; the farmer casually explains she's either awake or urinating
jokes.punchline: Moira treats being awake-or-urinating as a developmental achievement worthy of praise — 'great progress for Bebe'
jokes.explanation: Excellent. The farmer's blunt, earthy line ('takin' a leak') paired with Moira's patronizing congratulation ('great progress for Bebe') is a perfect collision of registers. 'Great progress for Bebe' treats the adult woman like a patient in a developmental program. Only Moira can deliver this with complete sincerity. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I came to this godforsaken farmhouse for what I thought would be a quaint weekend respite, and now I'm being held prisoner like some sort of political hostage in a third-world country!

jokes.text: Moira's explanation to Bebe: 'Yes, it seems he had taken ill, and stumbled out on the road poor thing, presumably to end it all.' John: 'And then?' Moira: 'And then we ran over him.'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to soften the news that they ran over the cat
jokes.punchline: She frames the cat as suicidal ('presumably to end it all') before matter-of-factly admitting they ran him over — the euphemism collapses into bluntness
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. 'Presumably to end it all' applied to a cat crossing a road is a spectacular piece of dark comedy — Moira constructs a narrative of feline suicidal ideation to soften blame, only for John to demand the actual ending, which she delivers flatly: 'And then we ran over him.' The structure is perfect — the euphemism builds, the straight-line deflates it. Only Moira could construct this logic and mean it sincerely. Approaches the calibration anchor for exceptional jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're telling me you want me to pay for a cat's funeral?

dialogue.speaker: Farmer
dialogue.line: That's right. Fluffy was a good mouser.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A good mouser? The cat is dead! What is it mousing now, the afterlife?

jokes.text: Moira, witnessing the family's grief: 'Anyway, this feels like a very private family moment...' [pause] '...that we're more than willing to share.'
jokes.setup: The family is having an emotional reaction to Sergio's death
jokes.punchline: Moira begins to excuse them gracefully then immediately reverses — she's staying
jokes.explanation: Exceptional construction. The setup ('very private family moment') promises social grace; the pause, then 'that we're more than willing to share' is a complete inversion of the expected sentiment. The joke works because the second half is grammatically correct but morally absurd. Highly quotable and a strong Moira character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's clearly not true. You've had multiple cats. I assume the oxygen deprivation from living in that car has affected your memory.

jokes.text: Roland returns to buy from the knockoff booth again, right in front of David and Stevie. Stevie: 'Clearly that brand loyalty you were talking about is really paying off.'
jokes.setup: David claimed his customers are too loyal to be fooled by a knockoff
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately proves him wrong a second time, and Stevie rubs it in with perfect deadpan
jokes.explanation: Sharp callback payoff. Stevie's delivery of 'really paying off' is the ideal deadpan punctuation. The joke works because Roland's obliviousness is perfectly consistent and Stevie's sarcasm is perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I want tickets to Hamilton.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You mean Hamiltrton?

jokes.text: David: 'I'd like to pay you off to not be here anymore.'
jokes.setup: Roland is at the booth being unhelpful and bothersome
jokes.punchline: David volunteers to pay Roland to leave — treating his presence as a quantifiable nuisance that has a price
jokes.explanation: Good David-Roland dynamic joke. The transaction framing ('pay you off') for social relief is a funny expression of David's economics-of-human-interaction mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why don't you take your grandmother to the spa?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's my mother.

jokes.text: David smells the knockoff foot cream: 'Ugh! It smells like pennies and burps!'
jokes.setup: David examines the knockoff 'Gel Time Moist Liquid' product Roland brought back
jokes.punchline: 'Pennies and burps' is a hilariously specific and unpleasant sensory description
jokes.explanation: Excellent sensory comedy. 'Pennies and burps' is genuinely inspired — both elements are familiar, unpleasant, and together they create a synesthetic horror. The specificity elevates what could be a throwaway disgust reaction into a memorable line. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, so there's Marcus, and then there's... who else?

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Well, there's Marcus, and then there's Derek.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Derek. Right. And that's...

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: And then there's Steve.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Steve. Okay. So Marcus, Derek, and Steve.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: And Gary.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's a Gary?

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: And Brian.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How many men are there?

jokes.text: Product reveal: 'Gel Time Moist Liquid. Contains 100% moist liquid.'
jokes.setup: David investigates the knockoff product's label
jokes.punchline: 'Contains 100% moist liquid' is a meaninglessly tautological product description — the product is liquid, its main ingredient is 'moist liquid'
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd. '100% moist liquid' as a product description is both a jab at meaningless wellness marketing and a perfect piece of fake-terrible-product comedy. The tautology (it's liquid, and that liquid is moist) is a precise comic construction. Highly quotable and works on rewatch for how much is packed into five words.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you and Antonio are... together?

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: What? No. He's my realtor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your realtor.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I thought you two were... I mean, the way you were talking about him...

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: David, he sold me a condo.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Uh-oh, Davey got busted!' followed by David insisting he's not selling the product while Roland suggests $3 for 15 is still a rip-off.
jokes.setup: The counterfeit product is exposed; Roland still doesn't understand the problem
jokes.punchline: Roland's concern is purely financial — not that it's counterfeit, but that $3 for 15 is overpriced for 'Gel Time'
jokes.explanation: Roland's inverted priorities are always funny. His consumer advocacy on behalf of terrible counterfeit foot cream is characteristically oblivious. 'Pharmacies hand those out for free on Halloween' is a nice specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Their apartment is so small, when Brad and Antonio have people over, someone always ends up in the bedroom.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: Last week, I was in there with three other people, and we were just... packed in there together.

jokes.text: Roland: 'I mean, pharmacies hand those out for free on Halloween.'
jokes.setup: Roland is explaining why $3 for Gel Time is a rip-off
jokes.punchline: The image of pharmacies handing out foot cream as Halloween candy is a specific and bizarre detail that implies Roland has direct personal experience with this
jokes.explanation: Excellent Roland line. The specificity of 'pharmacies hand those out for free on Halloween' creates a perfect character window — Roland is the kind of person who collects pharmaceutical handouts on Halloween. The image is specific enough to be genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Wendy
dialogue.line: I'm so grateful for everything you do for me, David. I don't know what I'd do without you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, you'd probably be lost. Literally and figuratively.

jokes.text: Customer at the knockoff booth: '$18 for toner? Why don't you just use some Gel Time? That stuff will strip your skin right off.'
jokes.setup: A customer at the knockoff booth complains about toner prices
jokes.punchline: She recommends Gel Time as a cheaper alternative while casually noting it will 'strip your skin right off' — the product is apparently dangerous
jokes.explanation: Good callback that extends the Gel Time joke. The casualness of 'strip your skin right off' implies this is a known property of the product that people accept — the horror delivered without alarm is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: So my grandmother, she actually died twice.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The first time was in 1987, but then she got better.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: And then the second time, it really stuck.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Very sudden. She was doing yoga.

jokes.text: $18 for toner? Why don't you just put on some Gel Time? That stuff will strip your skin right off.
jokes.setup: Roland is apparently still browsing/commenting on David's products
jokes.punchline: Roland recommends the industrial knockoff as a skincare alternative — with the secondary implication that it's chemical-grade and dangerous
jokes.explanation: Good running gag escalation. 'That stuff will strip your skin right off' as an endorsement of Gel Time as toner is dark and funny. Roland as consumer advisor is a good recurring bit.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I didn't break the sink, David. I was just washing my hair in it because the shower was taking too long, and then I may have accidentally kicked it, but that was after I was done washing my hair, so technically the sink was already broken by the time I kicked it.

jokes.text: David's reaction to watching the customer endorse Gel Time to another customer — implied horrified look at Stevie
jokes.setup: A customer recommends Gel Time (the terrible counterfeit product) unprompted
jokes.punchline: David's wordless horror as his legitimate store is undercut by a customer endorsing the skin-stripping knockoff
jokes.explanation: Inferred reaction beat — the 'Ooh' in the transcript likely marks David's horrified reaction. Functional visual comedy but mostly relies on performance rather than writing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? I wash my face in the sink sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Sometimes? Alexis, that is a sink. That is not a bathing facility.

jokes.text: Ted and Alexis's post-coital scene in David's store: 'Whoa! That was...' 'I know, like they're good, but that was like...' 'You pulled some new tricks out.' 'New to you.'
jokes.setup: Ted arrived for a delivery man roleplay; the scene cuts to them clearly having been intimate in the store
jokes.punchline: Their ellipsis-laden conversation implies they've just had sex in David's store — the understated mutual congratulation is the comedy
jokes.explanation: Good payoff on the roleplay setup. 'New to you' from Alexis is the standout line — simultaneously complimenting herself and mildly insulting Ted's awareness of her full range. The cringe element is that this happened in David's store while she was supposed to be watching it.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Anyway, David should leave you in charge of the store more often.' Alexis: 'Um... Ted, the door was unlocked the entire time!'
jokes.setup: Ted compliments Alexis's management of the store
jokes.punchline: Alexis breaks their satisfied mood by realizing the store was unlocked — anyone could have walked in during their encounter
jokes.explanation: Good cringe escalation. The unlocked door retroactively makes their entire encounter an exhibitionist risk. Ted's response ('They would've gotten quite the show!') reveals he's delighted rather than embarrassed, which adds character.

jokes.text: Ted and Alexis pretending there was a delivery man who broke the sink — Ted inventing increasingly implausible details about 'the scanner thing'
jokes.setup: David's store sink is broken after Ted and Alexis's encounter; they need a cover story
jokes.punchline: They construct a ridiculous story involving a drunk delivery man who broke the sink and left, which gets more absurd with each detail
jokes.explanation: The delivery man fiction coming full circle is clever — they're now actually lying about a delivery man. The 'scanner thing' bit is the funniest element, Ted committing to a detail he can barely name.

jokes.text: I'd get your signature, but I guess I left my clipboard and scanner thing somewhere, so.
jokes.setup: Alexis points out Ted never got a signature for the (fake) delivery
jokes.punchline: Ted commits to the fictional delivery persona — 'left my clipboard and scanner thing' — while both know there's no actual delivery
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the roleplay thread. 'Scanner thing' is a funny non-technical description that reveals Ted doesn't know what delivery equipment is actually called, which is oddly charming.

jokes.text: Ted: 'I guess I left my clipboard... and scanner thing somewhere, so.' Alexis: 'Do you think it might be in David's new bathroom? Apparently it's just for customers, but he never lets anyone use it.' Ted: 'I remember now, I definitely left my scanner thing in there.'
jokes.setup: They're constructing the cover story and need a reason to go to the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'scanner thing' — he can't even name the item — combined with Alexis giving him a perfect alibi that also jabs at David's bathroom policy
jokes.explanation: Two jokes woven together: Ted's inability to name his own props ('scanner thing') and the casual revelation that David has a customer-only bathroom he never lets anyone use. Both land efficiently.

jokes.text: Alexis suggests going to 'find the scanner thing'; Ted asks to drop the bit because 'it's kinda throwing me off'
jokes.setup: Alexis commits to the 'scanner thing' euphemism for going to the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Ted breaks the fiction to request they drop the bit — he's more comfortable with the explicit than the coy euphemism
jokes.explanation: A small meta moment where Ted's earnestness defeats Alexis's coyness. His preference for dropping the euphemism is a nice character beat — he'd rather be direct than perform.

jokes.text: Ted: 'Oh, this is nice!' [reacting to David's bathroom] then immediately: 'Uh-oh! Is that a sink? Oh! A sink it is!'
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis enter David's exclusive customer bathroom
jokes.punchline: Ted's exclamation about the sink implies he's immediately planning to repeat the incident that broke the previous one
jokes.explanation: Great escalation callback — they just broke one sink and are apparently about to break another. Ted's mock-surprised 'Is that a sink?' sells his enthusiasm with perfect comic energy. The escalation is implied and clean.

jokes.text: Moira at the farmhouse: 'How are we still trapped in this gothic horror show?'
jokes.setup: John and Moira are still at the farmhouse long after they hoped to leave
jokes.punchline: Moira frames a polite condolence visit as a 'gothic horror show' — theatrical catastrophizing at its peak
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable Moira line. 'Gothic horror show' for a farmhouse condolence visit is an inspired overstatement. The line is both self-aware (she knows she's being dramatic) and completely sincere. Strong character specificity.

jokes.text: John: 'You know, I think we've already missed the seaweed wrap, and at this rate, it's not looking good for the salt scrub.'
jokes.setup: They're still trapped at the farmhouse
jokes.punchline: John's priority is the spa treatments they're missing — he's cataloguing the loss of seaweed wraps and salt scrubs while sitting in a grief-stricken household
jokes.explanation: John's gentle version of Moira's self-absorption — his concerns are expressed with more propriety but are equally misplaced. 'Not looking good for the salt scrub' has a nice matter-of-fact resignation to it.

jokes.text: Farmer asks the Roses to pay for the cat's funeral. John: 'The funeral... for your cat?' Farmer: 'Well it was Baby's only cat.' Moira: 'Perhaps it's the lack of air, but I believe I saw three or four cats running around since this interrogation began.'
jokes.setup: The farmer asks the Roses to cover the cat's funeral expenses
jokes.punchline: Moira calls the visit an 'interrogation' (revealing her felt victimhood) while also noting she's counted three or four other cats — the 'only cat' claim is suspect
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one sentence. 'This interrogation' frames their polite visit as police brutality while Moira's careful observation of the cat population exposes the farmer's manipulation. Both elements work together and reveal Moira's sharp situational intelligence beneath the theatrics.

jokes.text: Farmer asks if Bebe likes the theatre. Moira offers complimentary partially-obstructed-view seats to Cabaret. Farmer: 'No! She likes the Kinky Shoes.'
jokes.setup: Moira tries to offer theatre tickets as compensation for the dead cat
jokes.punchline: The farmer's daughter likes 'Kinky Shoes' — he's mangling the title 'Kinky Boots' — while Moira's offer of 'partially-obstructed view' seats is its own joke about her level of generosity
jokes.explanation: Double joke. Moira offering partially-obstructed seats as if they're a gift shows her estimation of what the daughter deserves. The farmer saying 'Kinky Shoes' is a perfect malaproprism that reveals cultural distance while also making John's correction ('I believe it's Kinky Boots') a small moment of his own pedantry.

jokes.text: John: 'I believe it's Kinky Boots. And no.' — delivered as if the title correction is the more important clarification
jokes.setup: The farmer says his daughter likes 'Kinky Shoes'
jokes.punchline: John corrects the title before rejecting the premise — his priorities are slightly skewed
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Four words ('I believe it's Kinky Boots') and one word ('And no.') — the title correction precedes the relevant response, which makes John seem faintly ridiculous. Very clean structure.

jokes.text: John suggests a professional massage for Bebe. Farmer: 'I like massages.' Moira: 'So, now you're taking Bebe to the spa?' John: 'No...maybe Baby can take her, uh, grandmother.' Farmer: 'I'm her mother!'
jokes.setup: John tries to offer a massage as compensation
jokes.punchline: John assumes 'Baby' (the daughter's nickname) is a grandmother-aged woman; she's actually the mother — revealing how badly they've misread the family dynamics
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed misunderstanding joke. The comedy builds through the negotiation — the farmer saying 'I like massages' (inappropriate interjection), Moira's alarm at John inviting the farmer to the spa, then John's grandmother assumption. Each beat adds a layer.

jokes.text: Moira offering the spa to the farmer: 'And you are going to adore the rejuvenating facial peel.' Farmer: 'Can I keep the slippers?'
jokes.setup: Moira is now selling the spa experience to the farmer as compensation
jokes.punchline: The farmer's negotiation reduces to the most practical possible demand — keeping the slippers — revealing where her priorities are
jokes.explanation: The farmer's negotiation is perfectly bathetic — a facial peel is offered and her counteroffer is slippers. 'Can I keep the slippers?' is an endearingly practical question that deflates all of Moira's theatrical salesmanship. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: John giving the farmer his aftershave as a parting gift: 'This is my favourite aftershave. This is for you.'
jokes.setup: John is desperately trying to appease the farmer to leave
jokes.punchline: He gives away his favourite personal item as an exit bribe — the escalation from cattle (cat) funeral expenses to spa day to John's aftershave is a wonderfully absurd negotiation arc
jokes.explanation: The aftershave is a small, perfect detail. John's desperation has reached the level where he's giving away grooming products. 'This is my favourite aftershave' lands as both confession and resignation.

jokes.text: Wendy to David at the flea market: 'You know, when Antonio came to me with this idea, I wasn't sure there was a market for another general store. Everyone is doing them now.' David: 'Yeah, I was one of the first, but I get it.'
jokes.setup: Wendy explains Antonio's business pitch, not realizing his store is copying David's
jokes.punchline: David's pained 'I was one of the first, but I get it' — acknowledging his originality while being completely undermined in the conversation about his own rip-off
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. David is being polite in the face of evidence that his concept was stolen. 'But I get it' is pained resignation. Works best knowing David's pride in the store.

jokes.text: David: 'I don't wanna be the one to have to tell you this, but it's about your fiancé.' Wendy: 'You know Brad?' David: 'Who's Brad?' Wendy: 'My fiancé.' David: '...I thought you and Antonio were—' Wendy: 'Oh David... we're business partners.'
jokes.setup: David has assumed Wendy and Antonio are a couple (and has been building to exposing him as a criminal fiancé)
jokes.punchline: Wendy has an entirely separate fiancé named Brad — Antonio was always just the business partner — and David's dramatic revelation has been aimed at the wrong person entirely
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection payoff. The entire Wendy/Antonio plot thread resolves with a reveal that recontextualizes everything — the audience assumed Antonio was the fiancé too, so the reveal lands as a double twist. 'Oh David... we're business partners' is perfectly delivered as gentle correction. The structure is near-perfect: long setup, rapid-fire deflation.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, that's gonna make this a little easier.' — pivoting immediately to exposing Antonio as a criminal now that Wendy's romantic feelings are irrelevant
jokes.setup: David has just learned Wendy isn't romantically involved with Antonio
jokes.punchline: His immediate recalibration — 'a little easier' — shows David's pragmatic relationship with other people's emotional lives
jokes.explanation: Small but precise character beat. David's 'a little easier' is the perfect David response — not sympathetic, not dramatic, just efficient. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'Antonio said it was locally sourced.' Stevie: 'Sure. If you consider the dollar store local. These sell for 99 cents.'
jokes.setup: Wendy was told the Gel Time was 'locally sourced' as part of the business pitch
jokes.punchline: Stevie demolishes the artisanal framing with 'the dollar store' — the wellness-marketing language collides with bargain-bin reality
jokes.explanation: Sharp comic punctuation on the Gel Time thread. 'If you consider the dollar store local' is a precise, funny reframe. The 99-cent price (while being sold for multiples more) completes the con. Stevie's delivery is always the ideal vehicle for this kind of line.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'In my weaker moments, I've been tempted to cheat on poor, sweet Brad.' David: 'With Antonio?' Wendy: 'Well, obviously you picked up on the intense heat between us if you thought we were a couple.'
jokes.setup: Wendy is being told Antonio is a criminal and a bad business partner
jokes.punchline: She confesses she's been tempted to cheat on her fiancé with her business partner — and then takes David's (mistaken) assumption of a relationship as validation of the 'intense heat'
jokes.explanation: Good callback — Wendy uses David's wrong assumption as evidence of real chemistry. The callback elevates the earlier joke. Wendy's cheerful oversharing about potential infidelity is consistent with her character.

jokes.text: Wendy explaining Brad and Antonio's living situation: 'He and Antonio are roommates. They share this tiny one-bedroom together. And it is so crowded in there, they had to put the mirror on the ceiling.'
jokes.setup: Wendy explains that telling Brad about Antonio will be complicated
jokes.punchline: The practical problem of two grown men sharing a one-bedroom, with a ceiling mirror as the revealing detail — the mirror implies either cramped practicality or something more
jokes.explanation: The ceiling mirror detail is perfectly Wendy — she describes it as a practical solution to crowding while the audience reads it differently. 'It is so crowded in there, they had to put the mirror on the ceiling' is an unexpectedly subversive line delivered with complete innocence.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah. Best to shut all that down, I think.' then 'I literally don't know, Wendy.' (when asked what she'd do without him)
jokes.setup: Wendy asks what she'd do without David
jokes.punchline: David agrees with her self-assessment — 'I literally don't know' — but with a sincerity that makes it less self-deprecation and more genuine assessment of her poor choices
jokes.explanation: Small but sharp. 'I literally don't know, Wendy' accepts her dependency without comfort. Works as a quick character beat.

jokes.text: Wendy, for several reasons I think you need to say goodbye to Antonio.
jokes.setup: Wendy has described the Brad/Antonio living situation including the ceiling mirror
jokes.punchline: 'For several reasons' — David's understated acknowledgment that there are multiple red flags here, not just the fraud
jokes.explanation: 'For several reasons' is doing maximum comedic work in minimum space. The phrase encompasses the fraud, the possible Brad/Antonio relationship, Wendy's near-infidelity, and more — all in three words. Good economy.

jokes.text: Wendy: 'You wanna get high?' David: 'No, but thank you.'
jokes.setup: The conversation has wrapped up satisfactorily
jokes.punchline: Wendy's sudden pivot to offering drugs as a casual social gesture — and David's politely declining as if offered a cup of tea
jokes.explanation: Excellent button joke. Three words from Wendy and four from David, and it's a perfect character scene ending. Wendy's casual drug offer and David's 'No, but thank you' — treating marijuana like a cracker — is a lovely collision of their personalities. The politeness is what makes it.

jokes.text: Alexis's cover story to David: 'The delivery man came in, used the bathroom, somehow broke the sink, and then left without delivering the package.'
jokes.setup: David returns to find his store damaged; Alexis must explain
jokes.punchline: The cover story is technically a lie but also perfectly describes what happened — except she omits that she and Ted were the 'delivery man'
jokes.explanation: The cover story works as a recontextualization of everything we saw — it's not technically wrong, just selective. The audience's knowledge of the truth makes every element of the lie funny. Rewatch value because you appreciate the precision of the misdirection.

jokes.text: David pressing Alexis on the cover story: 'He felt drunk because he knocked over a lot of things.' Alexis: 'Um, no... Well, you were here for part of it.' 'Right, but I got here late... my grandmother passed away.' David: 'Oh God!' Alexis: 'A few years ago. And it's just been real tough to get over.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is embellishing the cover story and borrows Ted's earlier dead grandmother improv
jokes.punchline: The grandmother has now died 'a few years ago' rather than 'this morning' — Alexis can't even keep Ted's story straight, and then extends it with performative grief
jokes.explanation: Excellent multi-level callback. Alexis has borrowed Ted's grandmother death story but misremembered the timeline ('a few years ago'), making it both a worse lie and funnier. 'It's just been real tough to get over' layers on performative grief. The callback rewards attentive viewers and deepens the joke.

jokes.text: I think he meant, like, sorry I missed you. Because I was here the entire time. And I actually think that he was like a little bit drunk, because he knocked over a lot of things.
jokes.setup: David finds a note from 'the delivery man' saying 'sorry I missed you'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's increasingly elaborate cover story — the delivery man was drunk and knocked over 'a lot of things' — escalates in detail as it becomes less plausible
jokes.explanation: Alexis building an elaborate lie in real time — adding 'he was drunk' and 'knocked over a lot of things' — while David clearly disbelieves her is classic sitcom structure executed with Alexis-specific flavor. The escalating detail makes it funnier as it gets less plausible.

jokes.text: David reveals there are security cameras: 'Fortunately we had these security cameras installed... I don't really know how this works, Patrick went to that seminar.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been telling her cover story
jokes.punchline: David reveals the cameras — undoing the entire lie — then immediately undermines his own power move by admitting he doesn't know what to do next because Patrick is at the tax seminar
jokes.explanation: Strong structural callback. David's gotcha moment (security cameras) is immediately self-undermined when he reveals he doesn't know how cameras work because Patrick handles everything. The Patrick/tax-seminar callback from the opening pays off perfectly. Reveals David's dependency on Patrick even in a confrontation.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Honestly, I don't think you wanna do that.' [referencing the security footage]
jokes.setup: David threatens to show the insurance people the security footage
jokes.punchline: Alexis's calm 'I don't think you wanna do that' — which the audience knows is absolutely correct and absolutely in her own self-interest
jokes.explanation: Alexis's understatement is perfectly calibrated — she knows exactly why he shouldn't show the footage, and her calm delivery is funnier than any dramatic response would be.

jokes.text: David: 'You said you could trust me. First the Tamagotchis, now this! Get out of my store.'
jokes.setup: David has discovered what happened in his store
jokes.punchline: He places the sex-in-his-store incident on equal moral footing with the Tamagotchi deaths — the callback to the opening joke recontextualizes the episode's entire character arc
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The Tamagotchi callback completing the episode's arc is structurally beautiful. David equating 'sex in my store' with 'killed my virtual pets' is both absurdist and perfectly in character — his hierarchy of offenses is uniquely his. The callback rewards viewers who have been watching the whole episode. Near-perfect structure.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You can take the money for the sink out of what you were going to pay me for today.' David: 'I wasn't planning on paying you anything. Get out!'
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to negotiate the sink payment out of her day's earnings
jokes.punchline: David reveals he wasn't going to pay her at all — she's been working for free all day and now offers to subtract payment she was never getting
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed quick joke. Alexis's negotiating from zero is a perfect absurdity — she's offering to subtract payment she was never owed. David's 'I wasn't planning on paying you anything' is casual and devastating. Their sibling relationship dynamic compressed into two lines.

jokes.text: Ted: 'And if we're pointing fingers, David, I've done it on a lot of sinks and I've never had an issue.' David: 'When you say you've done it on a lot of sinks—' Ted: 'Out!'
jokes.setup: David is evicting everyone; Ted offers a defense
jokes.punchline: Ted's double-entendre defense ('done it on a lot of sinks') leads David to begin questioning him — then David himself gets cut off and thrown out, reversing the power dynamic for one beat
jokes.explanation: Excellent episode-ending joke. Ted's unintentional double entendre ('done it on a lot of sinks') creates an escalation as David begins to probe it — then the power inverts when David himself is cut off by his own exit command echoed back at him. The reversal is the punch, and it's earned. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: And if we're pointing fingers, David, I've done it on a lot of sinks and I've never had an issue.
jokes.setup: David is kicking Alexis and Ted out while expressing disappointment
jokes.punchline: Ted defends himself by claiming experience with sink-based encounters — 'I've done it on a lot of sinks and I've never had an issue' — an inadvertently revealing defense
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Ted's defense — 'I've done it on a lot of sinks' — is delivered with the earnestness of a sincere complaint about an unjust accusation, while being completely self-incriminating. The 'I've never had an issue' implies this is a regular practice with an otherwise good track record. The comedy lives in Ted's total sincerity and complete unawareness of how this sounds. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira’s afternoon plans are interrupted when they accidentally hit a cat. David runs into an old colleague at the flea market whose new business bears a striking resemblance to Rose Apothecary.
insight: Character escalation drives a quieter episode as the Rose family navigates social humiliation.
